OPINION · ENVIRONMENT & POWER ·

Concrete Over Commons

The Kushners' Albanian acquisition is not a business story. It is a parable — about who gets to enjoy the world's last wild places, and who gets to decide they are done being shared.

Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump have acquired a stretch of Albania's Sazan Island — pristine, largely untouched, preserved precisely because it was never handed to developers. They visited, as the privileged do, and enjoyed what nature and Albanian public custodianship kept intact. Then they purchased it. Not to preserve it. Not to open it. To wall it off behind the price of admission and call it a resort.

The Sustainability Sermon and the Concrete Foundation

This is the defining hypocrisy of Western elite environmentalism: attend the summits, sign the pledges, pose before the glaciers — then buy the coastline. The same class that lectures the Global South on deforestation and carbon emissions moves with quiet efficiency to convert the world's remaining commons into exclusive amenities. Sustainability, in this grammar, means sustaining access for those who can pay. The rest may watch from a distance, or not at all.

They enjoyed what was preserved for everyone. Then they bought it for themselves. That is not investment. That is enclosure.
Enclosure Has Always Worn a Respectable Face

England's great enclosures of the 17th and 18th centuries dispossessed millions of common land under the banner of efficiency and improvement. The language today is different — "development," "eco-resort," "world-class destination" — but the geometry is identical: what was shared becomes private; what was free becomes priced; what belonged to all becomes the amenity of a few. The Kushner acquisition is enclosure with a helicopter pad and a sustainability brochure.

A World Divided by Altitude

The resort model is not incidental to this story — it is the point. A resort does not welcome; it sorts. It draws a line between those whose net worth grants entry and those for whom the view is now a postcard they cannot afford. Albania's coastline belonged, in the deepest sense, to the Albanian people and to the shared inheritance of the earth. Its conversion into a luxury enclave is a vote — cast in concrete and capital — for a world organised by hierarchy rather than humanity.


There was another path. Preservation. A foundation. A public trust. An act of genuine generosity from people of extraordinary means toward the commons that made their visit possible. They chose differently. And in that choice is a philosophy — that the beautiful, the wild, and the irreplaceable belong not to the world, but to whoever arrives with the largest cheque.

Albania deserves better. The earth deserves better. And the rest of us — those who will never afford the room rate — deserve to say so plainly.

Editorial position of Global Public Opinions. Responses from all regions welcome

Albania established a state-owned company to take a stake in a $1.4 billion luxury resort project led by Jared Kushner, Donald Trump’s son-in-law.
The company, set up days after Kushner and Ivanka Trump visited the coastal city of Vlore, will oversee state assets and ensure a government say in the project’s development, operation and the use of its proceeds. It was registered Thursday as Albanian State Development & Real Estate.
Kushner is spearheading several projects in the Balkans, where leaders are competing to get into the good graces of Trump and his family.
Sazan, Albania’s only island, was off-limits during the country’s communist era. Kushner’s project plans to replace abandoned military structures with a marina, upscale residences, hotels and restaurants, developing roughly 8% of the island.
Pro-US Prime Minister Edi Rama is seeking to shift Albania’s rapidly expanding tourism from a mass model to luxury and sustainable development, with the Sazan resort a flagship investment. The  🦩 Revolution  is a massive, ongoing wave of anti-government protests in Albania. The civil unrest erupted in late May 2026 over a controversial luxury resort project backed by Affinity Partners, an investment firm led by Jared Kushner, the son-in-law of U.S. President Donald Trump. The movement began as a localized environmental protest on May 23, 2026, when residents and activists opposed construction preparation on Pishë-Poro beach in Zvërnec. The proposed multi-billion dollar project spans the uninhabited island of Sazan and the ecologically fragile Vjosa-Narta lagoon and delta. This area is a heavily protected sanctuary for more than 200 bird species—including over 1% of the global 🦩 population—as well as endangered loggerhead 🐢 and Mediterranean monk 🦭 . Protesters adopted the 🦩 as their primary mascot. Demonstrators regularly march with giant inflatable 🦩 , cardboard cutouts, and altered 🇦🇱 that replace the traditional black double-headed eagle with a 🦩 . Locals and activists accuse the government of illegally privatizing public lands and carving up a national wildlife reserve for foreign oligarchs and billionaires. The movement quickly morphed from an eco-campaign into a non-partisan, grassroots civic uprising. Driven largely by Gen Z and young people, the protests have spread from coastal villages directly into the capital city. Thousands have filled the streets of Tirana chanting "𝐀𝐥𝐛𝐚𝐧𝐢𝐚 𝐢𝐬 𝐧𝐨𝐭 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐬𝐚𝐥𝐞".  Mainstream national networks heavily censored the protests or aired AI-generated disinformation claiming the rallies were backed by foreign adversaries. This further fueled public fury regarding state capture and the erosion of local democracy. The " 🦩 Revolution" has reached an international scale, triggering major political and judicial reviews. The European Commission issued a stark warning to Tirana, stating that the project violates EU environmental protections and could jeopardize Albania's ongoing negotiations to join the European Union. Albania's Minister of Tourism and Environment, Sofjan Jaupaj, informed Brussels that construction has been temporarily halted pending a comprehensive environmental impact assessment. Albania's National Anti-Corruption Agency (SPAK) has officially launched an active criminal investigation into the legal status of the land and the entities linked to Kushner's development.

“Albanians are protesting against Rama, not against Trump.”

For several weeks, a small group of neo-Marxists and environmental activists had been protesting in Zvërnec, where a tourist resort linked to relatives of President Trump is planned to be built. Hardly anyone paid attention to them until the day security personnel dragged one of the protesters across the ground.

This brutal act of violence against the protesters served as a catalyst and ignited social media. The younger generation—Gen Z, shaped by these networks—poured into the streets of Tirana, protesting against the violence, in defense of the environment, against the lack of clarity surrounding the ownership of the land where the project is to be built, against the government’s abuse of strategic investment projects, against oligarchs, organized crime, and the government’s misuse of public assets for the benefit of its favored clients. Yes, there were also voices opposing the specific project itself.

As you can see, these are grievances caused by the government of Edi Rama. They have nothing to do with President Trump, his relatives, or the United States of America.

On the fifth day of the protest, the protesters came forward with political demands. First and foremost, they are calling for the resignation of Prime Minister Rama and his government. Other demands relate to ending government abuse of public investments, corruption, property theft, and so-called “strategic investments.”

Edi Rama belongs to the global left-wing network, and so does his government. International left-leaning media, for their own political reasons, rushed to frame this protest against the left-wing government as a protest against relatives of President Trump.

At the same time, once the protest began to articulate political demands—first and foremost the resignation of Rama—he came out and promoted the first falsehood, claiming that these protests were directed against President Trump rather than against him. There is nothing more untrue, malicious, or distorted than this claim by the Prime Minister.

The second falsehood was when, by exploiting the global geopolitical situation, he suggested that the protests were orchestrated by Iranians.

The second demand of the protesters is the repeal of the law on strategic investments. This law was passed by Edi Rama many years before relatives of President Trump even became aware of Albania.

It was adopted in order to reward and appropriate public property for the benefit of the Prime Minister’s personal clients.

The third demand of the protesters concerns the repeal of the “Mountain Package,” another initiative by Rama aimed at taking public property for the benefit of his clients in mountainous areas. Jared Kushner has no connection or interest in this initiative.

Unfortunately, it should be noted that the Prime Minister used both the initiative and Kushner’s project to set a trap for him.

I have never been more proud of my people than I have been over the last four days.
As many of you may have already seen in the news, Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner's investment plans in the Zvërnec area have sparked significant opposition across Albanians in Albania, New-York, Munich, Stockholm and London. They were drawn to our beautiful coastline, our hospitality, and the incredible potential of our country. 
What they failed to understand is that Albanians would never sell their land, identity, history, and future.
This nation has existed on its land for thousands of years. Long before modern states, long before borders were drawn and redrawn across Europe, our ancestors were here. 
We welcome investment, partnerships, and development, but there is a difference between investing in a country and transforming it into something its people no longer recognize.
Over the last four days, thousands of Albanians have shown that civic engagement is alive and well. Regardless of where one stands on the project itself, seeing people stand up peacefully for what they believe in is something that deserves respect.
A country's greatest wealth is not its coastline, its resorts, or its buildings. It is its people. And these past few days have reminded me just how wealthy I am.

#handsoffAlbania

One image. Ten days. A nation’s 🇦🇱 cry for dignity. A video of private security violently dragging a peaceful protester in Zvërnec, southern Albania, on May 30th was all it took. Within hours, thousands were in the streets. Within days, the world was watching.

What started as a reaction to a $4 billion luxury resort investment linked to Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump has become something far bigger and far more important to understand correctly.

This is NOT a protest against American 🇺🇸investment. Albania is the most pro-American nation in the Balkans. The U.S. Embassy is trusted more than its own government. What Albanians are demanding is transparency, accountability, and the right to be heard on decisions that shape their country’s future.

The economic stakes are real. Tourism now accounts for 26.4% of Albania’s GDP. The country welcomed 11.7 million visitors in 2024 in a nation of just 2.4 million people and expects over 12 million in 2025. Albania’s coastline is extraordinary. Bold investment is not the enemy. Closed-door decisions are.

But the deeper story is about governance and accountability. 

Prime Minister Edi Rama’s Socialist Party has won four consecutive terms, now controlling 83 of 140 parliamentary seats. OSCE observers flagged widespread intimidation in the May 2025 elections. Albania’s elite anti-corruption unit SPAK backed by the EU and the U.S. has opened a formal investigation into the title properties of the land where the resort is planned, raising questions who legally owns the ground on which a multi-million vision is being build. 

What is extraordinary about this movement is its breadth. Leftists, environmentalists, nationalists, and young families are marching together peacefully, and without the backing of any political party. They clean the streets after every protest. They are also targeting opposition leader Sali Berisha, designated persona non grata by the United States in 2021 for corruption. They want both men gone.

And the story has gone global for reasons worth examining.

The Kushner and Trump name has attracted a remarkably broad coalition of international actors: anti-Trump movements, left-wing ecological networks across Europe, and others with their own agendas. These are not all fake news. They are different actors exploiting different angles around a small country’s local story. And there is a feedback loop real Albanian grievances are being amplified externally, and those narratives feed right back into the streets of Tirana.

Albania has 296 miles of stunning Adriatic and Ionian coastline, locked away behind the Iron Curtain for fifty years. It deserves transformative investment. It also deserves full transparency. Those two things are not in conflict.

I have been covering Albania for 30 years. The Flamingo Revolution 🦩 is the most significant civic movement this country has seen since the fall of communism in 1990

I rarely write about political issues. Having lived outside Albania for the past seven years, I don't consider myself close enough to the country's political landscape to support one party or another. This is why what I am about to say is not political.
It is simply the reaction of an Albanian who feels heartbroken.

Over the last few days, I have been following the discussions surrounding Narta Lagoon, Sazan Island, and the development projects planned in these areas. The more I read, the more concerned I become, not because development is inherently bad, but because I struggle to find clear answers to very simple questions.

Like many others, I learned about the involvement of Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner in projects connected to Sazan Island. My concern is not with investors. Investors will always pursue opportunities. My concern is whether Albania is protecting its own interests with the same determination that it welcomes investment.

Narta Lagoon is not just another piece of land. It is one of Albania's natural treasures, home to hundreds of species and an ecosystem of extraordinary value. Places like this do not belong to politicians, investors, or governments. They belong to Albania.🇦🇱 

What troubles me most is the lack of transparency. Citizens are asking reasonable questions about environmental impact, biodiversity, and the future of these areas. They deserve clear answers. Instead, many feel unheard.

I am equally troubled by how peaceful citizens are facing pressure simply for raising concerns and by the apparent silence of parts of the media on an issue that deserves public discussion.

Perhaps the final project will be successful. Perhaps every promise will be fulfilled. I genuinely hope so. But trust is built through transparency, not through uncertainty.

As someone living abroad, I do not pretend to know every detail. But I know enough to feel concerned, for the citizens who feel ignored, for future generations who may never experience these places as we know them today, and for my own children, who deserve to inherit an Albania that protects its natural heritage.

This is not about left or right. It is not about being pro-government or anti-government.

It is about Albania.🇦🇱 
Albania deserves answers. 
Albanians deserve transparency.
And Albania's natural treasures deserve protection.

Because Albania is not for sale.🇦🇱

Albania’s luxury coastal plan linked to Trump sparks storm – why are people protesting?

Thousands of people have flooded the streets across Albania to protest against a major coastal development project linked to Jared Kushner, son-in-law of US President Donald Trump. The demonstrations, among the largest seen so far, come as opposition builds against plans to construct luxury resorts along the country’s Adriatic coastline.In the capital Tirana, thousands gathered peacefully on Saturday evening. Earlier the same day, several hundred people also protested in Zvernec....

ON THE ALBANIAN PROTESTS: WHY NOW?

Since the area around Zvernec was fenced off, and local protests against the tourism projects in Narte, Sazan, and Zvernec began on May 23rd, the mood in Albania has suddenly shifted. The protests have grown and have taken the capital, Tirana, with crowds growing each day. The international media is catching up, although the framing has been too focused on Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump because that story has legs. But the protests are about something much larger; they’re about the systematic abuse of property rights in Albania, dispossession, the corruption of the elite in both major parties, and an affordability crisis that is sending its young people abroad in droves. 

Zvernec was the straw that broke the camel’s back, but this story has been years in the making, and what you are seeing now is the culmination of resentment and anger that have been seething beneath the surface. The Albanian government has been very good at selling a glitzy image abroad, but, for most of its citizens, that does not fit with their experience at home.

"The European Commission has cautioned Albania against actions that may impact its EU accession path, amid ongoing national protests over a Jared Kushner-linked development project on the country’s southern coast.

"National protests entered their seventh consecutive day on Sunday as Albanians demanded the cancellation of a luxury resort linked to U.S. President Donald Trump’s son-in-law, arguing that the project threatens a protected wildlife area home to flamingos, seals, and turtle nesting sites.

"The Commission is warning that the project could put Albania on a collision course with the EU’s environmental rules, jeopardizing its ability to close the green Chapter 27 in its accession talks.

"“Albania should refrain from actions that could undermine the fulfillment of the closing benchmarks and [we] expect the Albanian authorities to act without delay,” a European Commission spokesperson told POLITICO in response to a question about the controversial proposed development.

"“In the EU accession process, as part of the closing benchmarks for negotiating Chapter 27 on environment and climate change, Albania is expected to align fully with EU legislation in this area, including the Birds and the Habitats Directives,” said the spokesperson, urging that Albania repeal the changes to the Law on Protected Areas and “terminate” the law on strategic investments. As part of the process of joining the EU, Albania is expected to align with the EU’s environmental rules.

"The spokesperson also told POLITICO that Albania’s environment minister, Sofjan Jaupaj, had informed the Commission in Brussels that construction on the project has been suspended and an environmental impact assessment will be carried out with civil society. …

"Dubbed the “Flamingo Revolution” after the flamingos that reside in the protected area, the protests intensified over the weekend, with calls growing for Prime Minister Edi Rama to resign. "Albanians in New York, London, Brussels, Milan and Berlin, as well as other cities in Albania, have joined the calls for the project to be scrapped ...

"Albania is a frontrunner to join the EU alongside Montenegro, and Rama has set a target of joining the bloc by 2030, with technical negotiations to close at the end of 2027.

"The scrutiny comes because of Albania’s 2024 changes to its Protected Areas law, which critics say opened the door to large-scale development in sensitive wildlife areas. ...

"Brussels has also long pressed Tirana to scrap its 2015 Strategic Investments law, as it gives favored projects fast-track treatment that risks bypassing EU environmental safeguards."

Satir och flamingos driver Albaniens nya proteströrelse

Albaniens så kallade Flamingo Revolution har vuxit från en lokal miljöprotest till en bredare politisk mobilisering mot premiärminister Edi Ramas regering. Protesterna riktas mot ett planerat lyxresortprojekt nära det skyddade Vjosa-Narta-området och på Sazanön, där investerare med koppling till Jared Kushner och Ivanka Trump pekas ut som drivande bakom planerna (Balkan Insight, 2026a; Reuters, 2026a).

Rörelsen har fått stor spridning genom satiriska plakat, memes och rosa flamingos som politiska symboler. Skyltarna driver med regeringen, utländska investerare och bilden av Albanien som ett land där kust, natur och offentliga intressen kan säljas ut. Protesthumorn fungerar därmed både som gatupolitik och som material för sociala medier (Balkan Insight, 2026b).

Bakgrunden är Vjosa-Nartas känsliga kustekosystem, med flamingos, havssköldpaddor, munksäl och många fågelarter. The Guardian rapporterar att området ännu saknar fullständigt miljötillstånd och planeringstillstånd, samtidigt som maskiner och avspärrningar redan synts på platsen. Regeringen beskriver det som tekniska förberedelser, medan kritiker ser ett försök att skapa fakta på marken (The Guardian, 2026).

Protesterna tog fart efter uppgifter om avspärrningar, markarbeten och konfrontationer mellan demonstranter och privat säkerhetspersonal vid Zvërnec. Därefter spreds protesterna till Tirana, där parollen ”Albania is not for sale” blev samlande. Rörelsen riktas därför inte bara mot resortprojektet, utan också mot upplevd korruption, bristande insyn och regeringens hantering av allmänna naturresurser (Balkan Insight, 2026c; OBCT, 2026).

Edi Rama har sagt att projektet ska fortsätta och framställer det som en del av Albaniens modernisering och turismstrategi. Reuters uppger att projektet nära Vjosa-Narta uppskattas till cirka 1,4 miljarder euro. Rama har avfärdat kraven på att stoppa planerna, men medgett att användningen av taggtråd vid platsen var ett misstag (Reuters, 2026a).
Frågan har även fått en EU-dimension. EU-kommissionen har varnat Albanien för att miljölagstiftning och skyddade områden måste hanteras i linje med landets EU-anslutningsprocess. Det gör Flamingo Revolution till mer än en lokal protest. Den handlar om rättsstat, miljöskydd, turismmodell och relationen till mäktiga utländska investerare (Reuters, 2026b; The Guardian, 2026).

Jared Kushner-Linked Resort Project in Albania Faces Investigation and Growing Protests

A luxury tourism development project linked to Jared Kushner, the son-in-law of President Donald Trump, is facing increased scrutiny in Albania as anti-corruption prosecutors investigate changes to the protected status of a coastal wetland where the project is planned.

📍 Albania's Special Anti-Corruption Prosecution Office (SPAK) has reportedly launched an investigation into modifications made to the protected designation of the Vjosa-Narta coastal landscape near Zvërnec.

The area is known for its environmental significance and serves as a habitat for flamingos, Mediterranean monk seals, and sea turtle nesting sites.

Kushner's investment firm, Affinity Partners, has previously discussed plans to develop luxury tourism projects in Albania. Reports indicate the proposed development could include thousands of hotel rooms and villas along the country's coastline.

Albanian Prime Minister Edi Rama has confirmed that discussions regarding the project are ongoing, describing tourism investment as part of Albania's broader economic development strategy.

Meanwhile, the project has sparked growing opposition from environmental groups and local residents.

🚨 Demonstrations intensified after fencing was erected around portions of the proposed development area, limiting public access to sections of the coastline.

Recent protests have also generated controversy after videos appeared to show confrontations between demonstrators and private security personnel. Albanian authorities have reportedly taken action against security companies involved while investigations continue.

‘I Grew Up Trying To Explain the Truth About Albania—the Sazan Island Protests Are About So Much More Than Ivanka and Jared’s Resort’ by Kelsi Karruli

When Ivanka Trump opened up this month about the $1.4 billion resort she and Jared Kushner are building on an island in Albania, she marveled about the "unbelievable" beauty they had witnessed when visiting the site for the first time—while vowing to protect that landscape even as they construct a 10,000-room development atop it.

Yet just hours after her gushing interview about the project, which is being developed on the island of Sazan, the site of a former military weapons base, a very different reality unfolded as thousands of Albanians took to the streets to protest the Trump-Kushner planned resort.

The juxtaposition of Ivanka's picture-perfect description of the development with the photos of its most vehement critics spoke volumes to me. Not just about the resort, or even the island of Sazan, but about the way in which Albania has so long been viewed by the Western world: as a place to be discovered, developed, and defined by outsiders rather than the people who call it home. 

For as long as I can remember, Albania felt like a place I was constantly translating—not just linguistically, but culturally. 

To my family, it was home. To many Americans, it was a country they couldn't locate on a map, or understood only through stereotypes. Stuck somewhere between those two realities, I found myself trying to bridge the gap.

My parents left Albania chasing the same thing countless immigrants before them had pursued: the American dream. But the version they found was far less glamorous than the one they imagined from across the Atlantic.

My father spent his days riding a bike to work in the pouring rain, traveling miles to a broken trailer with missing windows, doing whatever he could to build a life from scratch. He arrived in the U.S. with little more than hope, determination, and the belief that sacrifice would eventually lead to something better.

In many ways, it did.

For as long as I can remember, Albania felt like a place I was constantly translating—not just linguistically, but culturally. (Pictured: Tirana) (Kelsi Karruli)

When Ivanka Trump opened up this month about the $1.4 billion resort she and Jared Kushner are building on an island in Albania, she spoke about its "unbelievable" beauty, something I have spent years trying to help Americans see.  (YouTube/David Senra)

Yet decades later, despite building careers, raising families, paying taxes, and contributing to the country my parents chose as home, they still encounter questions about citizenship. Their accents still invite assumptions.

📣 VICTORY IUCN Members Confirm: Protected Areas Are For Nature! 🌿 #policy

Just ahead of the 2025 IUCN World Conservation Congress, members voted overwhelmingly in favor of Motion 130, calling for stronger restrictions on unsustainable #tourism infrastructure in the world’s most sensitive protected areas (IUCN Categories I & II).

The motion was initiated by Albanian NGO PPNEA and co-sponsored by 14 organisations from 13 countries—including EuroNatur Foundation, BirdLife International  and Wetlands International.

It began as a response to controversial legal changes in #Albania (Law No. 21/2024) which opened the door to large-scale tourism and energy projects — even in (!) national parks. 

Now Motion 130 has become a global statement: 

❗ Nature protection must not be undermined by development disguised as tourism
❗ Tourism must respect ecological limits
❗ Protected areas are not blank spaces for infrastructure

🌍 This vote also reinforces the European Union’s demand that Albania restore environmental safeguards as part of its #EUAccession process.

JOSHUA LIM  (photo 2); The mayors and residents of the Shushica Valley are protesting against the Himara Water Extraction Project. The Shushica River is one of the Vjosa's main tributaries. In March 2023, the Vjosa was designated Europe's first Wild River National Park,

A similar project is planned for the nearby, uninhabited Sazan Island, which lies within a marine national park.

After the post I shared yesterday on building Hotels on a coastline, I received many DMs asking what was I really talking about.

Well...

Jared Kushner’s private equity firm, Affinity Partners, is advancing plans for a massive €4 billion master-planned luxury tourism development on the Zvërnec Peninsula and Sazan Island 🇦🇱. 

While the Albanian government champions the project as a ticket to the "Champions League of global tourism,"  the venture finds itself in a perfect storm of regulatory, environmental, and legal crises. 

Here is a strategic breakdown of what’s happening on the ground:
1. The €4 Billion Corporate Pipeline 💸
The local entity spearheading the project, Zvërnec South Adriatic Development SH.P.K., relies on a complex network of offshore holding structures. The venture connects Kushner’s Affinity Partners—largely financed by Middle Eastern sovereign funds—with prominent Qatari co-investors (the billionaire Al-Khayyat brothers) and powerful local Albanian conglomerates. 

2. Ecological Friction & Public Backlash 🦩
The development sits within the Piše Poro–Narte protected area, a critical biodiversity sanctuary in Europe. 
The Fencing Controversy: The tension escalated dramatically when metal fences were erected across 300 hectares of dunes, pine forests, and beaches. This sparked direct clashes between local residents and private security guards, resulting in arrests, the revocation of security licenses, and the restructuring of local police leadership. 

Legislative Overrides: To pave the way, the Albanian parliament rushed through Law 21/2024, which effectively allows five-star resorts to bypass previous environmental protections. Conservation groups like PPNEA and EuroNatur are actively challenging these changes in court. 

3. Judicial Scrutiny & Land Title Risks ⚖️
Perhaps the most volatile risk is the active property investigation. Albania's Special Prosecution Office (SPAK) executed a series of raids targeting a systematic, multi-thousand-hectare land-title forgery and money-laundering scheme in the Zvërnec area. High-profile local intermediaries, including engineers and former lawyers linked to the development's land acquisitions, are currently under intense judicial scrutiny. 

The Takeaway:
Modern real estate megaprojects are no longer just about concrete and luxury hospitality. They are complex geopolitical instruments. For global investors and developers, Albania serves as a masterclass in why robust ESG, local title diligence, and geopolitical risk management are more critical than ever.

Will the €4 billion Zvërnec project redefine Mediterranean luxury, or will it succumb to legal and environmental crosswinds?

How Albania Is Being Sold Off, Piece by Piece, to Billionaires

There is something deeply revealing about the argument that surfaces every time another protected area in Albania is opened to luxury investors: “Albanians never went there anyway.”

Some said it about Sazan Island when the Albanian government granted preliminary approval for a luxury resort project linked to Jared Kushner, Donald Trump’s son-in-law. Others say it today about the wetlands surrounding Narta Lagoon and the ecosystems connected to Divjakë-Karavasta National Park.

As if nature’s value depends solely on whether people consume it.

A forest does not need to become a resort to justify its existence. A lagoon does not need beach clubs, villas, or marinas to matter. An island does not need to be “activated” by billionaires to become valuable. Some places deserve protection simply because they are ecosystems, habitats, migratory corridors, and living environments for species already facing extinction.

The notion that every untouched landscape is merely “unused real estate” is one of the most destructive ideas of modern development. Wrapped in the language of investment and growth, it reduces nature to a commodity and treats biodiversity as an obstacle rather than an asset.

The Karaburun-Sazan Marine National Park is not empty land waiting for profitable civilization to arrive. It is one of the Mediterranean’s most fragile and ecologically significant marine ecosystems. Environmental groups have repeatedly warned that the proposed luxury resort threatens habitats used by endangered species, including the Mediterranean monk seal and numerous protected marine organisms.

The same applies to the Vjosa-Narta Protected Landscape and the ecosystems linked to Divjakë-Karavasta. These are not simply picturesque coastlines. They form part of one of Europe’s most important migratory bird corridors and provide refuge to more than 200 bird species, including many that are threatened at a global level. Divjakë-Karavasta also contains one of the last intact delta ecosystems in the Mediterranean basin.

Yet bulldozers have entered these landscapes. According to environmental organizations, construction activities, dune destruction, forest clearing, and interventions inside protected areas have raised serious concerns regarding environmental safeguards, transparency, and public consultation.

This should concern anyone who believes that European integration is about more than economic indicators. The rule of law, environmental protection, and democratic accountability are supposed to be central pillars of the European project.

At a time when Albania is being praised for progress on its path toward EU membership, an uncomfortable question remains: how can a country move closer to Europe while some of its most valuable natural heritage is being opened to exclusive investment projects that primarily benefit global elites?

The answer will define not only Albania’s coastline, but also the….

WHAT IS HAPPENING IN ALBANIA?

For the second day, thousands have gathered in Tirana to protest against the regime. The trigger? Approval of a luxury tourism project on Sazan Island, a protected national park.

The project received Strategic Investor Status, which grants special privileges and fast-tracked procedures under Albanian law. The designation was given to Atlantic Incubation Partners LLC, a company linked to Jared Kushner’s business network. The plan involves a €1.4 billion luxury resort spanning 45 hectares of Sazan Island. The committee that approved the status was chaired by Edi Rama himself. 

Sazan Island is part of the Karaburun-Sazan Marine National Park, one of Albania’s most important protected ecosystems. Environmental groups say the area is vital for biodiversity, including the Mediterranean monk seal, one of the world’s rarest marine mammals. Meanwhile, the nearby Vlorë-Narta Lagoon faces growing pressure from two major developments: the government-backed Vlorë International Airport and large-scale tourism projects in the Vjosa-Narta and Sazan areas linked to Jared Kushner’s business.

The lagoon is one of Europe’s most important wetlands and a key part of the Vjosa Delta ecosystem. It hosts more than 200 bird species and serves as a major stopover for migratory birds in the Mediterranean, including flamingos and pelicans.

Two days ago, dozens of citizens and environmental groups held a peaceful protest against the project in the Narta Lagoon. They were attacked by masked men backed by the regime, and one of the protesters was abducted in broad daylight in front of police officers, who stood by and shielded the attackers instead of intervening. 

The violence has fueled even larger protests in Tirana. For two days, thousands have taken to the streets, and demonstrations are set to continue tomorrow and throughout the entire week. Mainstream media is not covering the protests, but people are organizing through social media. Public support is also growing, with artists, intellectuals, and other prominent figures openly standing with the protesters.

For the first time, former Prime Minister Sali Berisha has not tried to co-opt the movement. Because the project is linked to the Trump family, and Berisha has increasingly aligned himself with Trump-style politics, he has publicly backed the investment and is unable to oppose it. As a result, many have turned against him as well. 

Under the slogan “Albania Is Not for Sale,” protesters are calling for the project to be canceled and for Rama to resign.

On this #WorldEnvironmentDay, let's not plant trees.

Instead, let's speak plainly.

What is happening in Albania, in the name of “tourism,” is not progress. 

It is grief for Sazan Island, for the Vjosa-Narta Lagoon, for the Dalmatian pelican, the loggerhead turtle, and for the centuries of history, biodiversity, and belonging that are being repackaged into nice tourist brochures for billionaires.

With an Albanian government, eager to trade even its sovereignty for short-term prestige, Jared Kushner, son-in-law of Donald Trump, has been granted Strategic Investor Status to build a €1.4 billion luxury resort on Sazan Island, a once-protected military and ecological jewel.

No trees this day. Let's plant some words.

- Sazan lies within a National Marine Park.
- Zvernec, the next target, is inside a protected wetland.
- The Vjosa Delta is not just Europe’s last untouched river delta. It is a sacred artery of life, home to endangered species, fragile ecosystems, and voices that have no lobbyists, governmental officials or billionaires to speak for them.

And while Albania declares its commitment to EU values and biodiversity protection on one hand, with the other it warms up the bulldozers, or better to say, outsources them.

Jared Kushner arrives not with humility, but with billion dollar ambitions. His plans, wrapped like a souflaki in the language of “sustainable development,” promise private villas, a luxury resort, and a cost measured not in euros, but in irreversible ecological loss.

I understand that many are excited. Albania, once overlooked by global investors, now attracting billions. 

But few understand this is not investment. Not opportunity, but robbery of nature, of heritage, and of Albania’s soul.

The government has been efficient. New laws now permit construction in protected areas. But let us be clear: It is not law that will destroy the Vjosa Delta.

It is the silence of Albanians.

Let's plant a voice!

Few understand that Albanians are failing in their duty as protectors of a European treasure, seduced by the flattery of being called the "Maldives of Europe", while our beautiful coastline is carved up like a private backyard.

This is not just about Albania. It is about Europe’s credibility. Because a continent that cannot protect its last wild river has no moral standing to lecture the world on green values.

When I was a child, we had a swallow’s nest on our small balcony, like meany Albanians families. It was sacred, never touched, because the swallow would leave in autumn and return in spring.

If we destroy our protected lands, there will be no return.

If we remain silent today, tomorrow we will stand on artificial beaches, look back at artificial lagoons, and wonder where the real world went.

The flashpoint in Sazan Island and the Vjosa Narta lagoon on Albania’s Adriatic coast, where Jared Kushner’s Affinity Partners plans a 1.4 billion euro luxury resort. The project covers the uninhabited island and an undeveloped stretch of coastline next to a wetland that hosts flamingos, Mediterranean monk seals and sea turtle nesting sites. Bulldozers and fencing appeared at the site in late May, triggering local protests that spread to Tirana. Demonstrators held pink flamingo cutouts and chanted that Albania is not for sale, accusing Prime Minister Edi Rama’s government of fast tracking a deal that sidelines environmental rules and public consultation. Over 40 environmental groups have petitioned to halt construction, saying the development would fragment critical habitat for migratory birds and disrupt tourism that already supports coastal communities.

The government argues the resort can coexist with nature and will bring jobs and foreign investment to a region that has remained largely untouched since communism. Rama says an environmental impact assessment is underway and that the project involves architects and investors from several countries, not just the Kushner family. Affinity Partners has pledged responsible stewardship and habitat enhancement, pointing to its plan for low density development and conservation funding. But Albania’s anti corruption agency has opened a probe into the legal changes that reclassified parts of the protected area in 2024, and opposition lawmakers are calling for the deal to be scrapped. For many Albanians, the fight has become less about tourism and more about who controls public land and whether protected status means anything when high profile investors are involved.

Geopolitical Analysis Breakdown
--------------------
Chokepoint Geopolitics & Dual-Use Infrastructure
--------------------
The transformation of Sazan Island (Saseno) from a Cold War military outpost into a luxury resort by Jared Kushner’s Affinity Partners is a textbook example of "soft power" overlapping with hard security. In international relations, commanding a naval chokepoint like the Strait of Otranto means commanding the entry and exit of the entire Adriatic Sea. Modern luxury developments require deep-water docks, advanced telecommunications, and private surveillance systems—all of which can serve as dual-use infrastructure easily adapted for intelligence gathering and maritime monitoring.
Energy Security and the TAP Pipeline
Your insight regarding the Trans Adriatic Pipeline (TAP) is highly accurate. The TAP is a critical artery for European energy diversification away from Russian gas, running from Azerbaijan to Italy. The subsea pipeline enters the Adriatic just north of Sazan Island.
Hybrid Warfare Shield: Following the Nord Stream sabotages, the security of underwater energy infrastructure has become paramount. A private, highly secured island fortress in close proximity to the pipeline provides a permanent vantage point to monitor both surface and subsea activities (such as drone or submarine movements).
Geopolitical Leverage: By anchoring a multi-billion-dollar American-backed asset right next to Europe’s southern energy corridor, the investment establishes a strategic US footprint. This grants significant diplomatic and economic leverage over the energy security of Italy and the wider European Union.

I'm reading about the protests in Albania against the $1.6bn Kushner/Ivanka Trump-linked resort planned for Sazan Island alongside the FT's recent analysis of maritime chokepoints, which we were discussing at Starling Institute ("The power struggle in the world's narrow seas," May 24, link below)... and a second layer to the environmental case comes into view, one that should interest Italians as much as Albanians.

The FT maps a world where chokepoints have become instruments of leverage, as Hormuz is demonstrating right now. Sazan faces Italy and sits at the mouth of the Strait of Otranto, the only maritime gateway between the Adriatic and the rest of the world. Everything moving by sea from and to Trieste, Venice, Ravenna (casa!) passes through it. The TAP pipeline carrying Azerbaijani gas to Europe crosses the seabed nearby. Sazan was a military asset for a century precisely because of where it sits.

A resort is not a naval base, and I'm wary of conspiracy framings. But strategic geography raises the bar for transparency, and that's where this project falls short. The protesters' question "who benefits, and who decided?" is basic governance, not paranoia. Italy, the other shore of the strait, should be asking it too.

There's a longer history here worth remembering. The world's chokepoints were among the first things states ever learned to govern by common rules: the 1888 Constantinople Convention guaranteed passage through Suez "in time of war as in time of peace"; the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (1982) turned strait access into a right no coastal state can suspend, precisely because nobody could be trusted to own a gateway alone. Hormuz is showing us, in real time, what it costs when that framework gives way.

Former Voice of America Director: The Protests Are Not Anti-Trump, but Against Government Theft; Rama Faces a Legitimacy Crisis

The protests were triggered by concerns over the non-transparent conditions surrounding high-profile investments linked to Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner. However, the demonstrations are not a referendum on the Trump family, nor are they a rejection of foreign capital. Albanians have long welcomed investments that respect the country’s laws, the environment, and the public interest. What they reject is the way these projects were pushed forward: without transparency, without consultation, and without respect for environmental or property rights. The protests are not anti-Trump or anti-American; they are against kleptocracy.

For years, Rama governed as if political competition were an annoyance rather than a democratic necessity. Power became increasingly centralized. Parliament functioned as a formality. Cabinet changes were announced in a theatrical manner, with ministers learning about their appointments during public speeches. Internal life within the ruling party narrowed to loyalty and conformity. The opposition, fragmented and demonized, was treated as a nuisance rather than a counterweight.

What makes this civic uprising extraordinary is its composition. It is not orchestrated by a political party. It is not driven by a single ideology. It is a coalition of students, environmental activists, property owners, workers, pensioners, and young professionals. Many of them had long disengaged from politics, convinced that participation carried little weight. Now they are in the streets, united by the shared belief that Albania cannot continue on its current path.

The controversy over the projects in Zvërnec and Sazan brought deeper grievances to the surface. For years, Albanians have watched public tenders, infrastructure contracts, and strategic assets being handed over to politically connected networks. High-profile corruption cases, such as the Balluku affair and the enrichment of ministers, senior officials, and their families, have reinforced the perception of a governing elite protected from accountability. Meanwhile, ordinary citizens have faced stagnant wages, rising costs, and declining public services. Nearly one million Albanians have emigrated over the past two decades, a staggering exodus that reflects both economic hardship and a loss of confidence in the country’s direction. The protests are, in part, a response to this long erosion of trust.

To understand what is happening in Zvërnec, with the project involving Jared Kushner, the son-in-law of U.S. President Trump, we need to go back in time. We need to go to the Dutch island of Aruba, where in 2019 a “summit” was held among Albanian crime bosses who divided Albania among themselves as spoils.

The Aruba meeting will not remain forever in the shadows. Foreign authorities possess a case file that has been forwarded to Albania. The main protagonist in this file is Artur Shehu, a controversial figure in organized crime who connects many threads.

Through criminal mechanisms, Shehu has allegedly managed to appropriate thousands of hectares of land not only in Zvërnec but also along Albania’s southern coastline, a matter that has remained uninvestigated to this day. If there were a full and comprehensive investigation, officials, businessmen, and even politicians would likely end up behind bars.

Returning to Zvërnec: in 2024, amendments were made to the law on Protected Areas, paving the way for the construction of luxury villas and hotels.

That same year, the company “Zvërnec South Adriatic Development” was established. It is owned through a chain of shell companies managed by the Dutch trust “Dutch Trust Management B.V.”

One of the offshore companies in this chain, “Blue Industries Investment Holding B.V.,” is owned by five anonymous individuals from Albania, whose identities are legally protected because none of them owns more than 25% of the shares (source: BIRN).

This is the key to the entire story. Who are these five Albanian shareholders? Who are these five individuals, and which of Rama’s close associates attended that meeting? The answer can be found if the Aruba meeting is investigated. To uncover the truth, the investigation must begin at the source and follow the threads that led to what is happening today.

What happened on Saturday in Zvërnec is unacceptable. It represents state capture and the barbaric face of a system of governance that, in all likelihood, had its own representative in Aruba.

I recently had the opportunity to join a TV panel to discuss one of Albania’s biggest current issues: the movement often referred to as the “Flamingo Revolution”.

International media is now beginning to pay attention, although much of the coverage has focused on Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump, because that aspect of the story is more immediately recognisable internationally. But the protests, I believe, are about something much larger.

One of the points I raised was that although Albania amended its national legislation in February 2024, domestic legal changes do not erase international obligations. Albania remains bound by international environmental principles that protect the public’s right to access environmental information, participate in environmental decision-making, and seek justice in environmental matters.

A further dimension concerns the protection of property rights, the dispossession of local communities, elite capture, weak accountability, and an affordability crisis that continues to push Albanians abroad — not only the young.

In addition, Zvërnec and Nartë may have been the straw that broke the camel’s back, but this frustration has been building for years. That is why this issue cannot be reduced to tourism, investment, or nationalism. I shall insist regarding the latter one because there is a revealing irony in the nationalist framing of this issue. Some Greek nationalist narratives try to present the movement mainly as a Greek minority question, while some Albanian nationalist narratives try to discredit it as foreign interference against Albania’s development. In different ways, both approaches weaken the movement by obscuring the real questions. 

In the final analysis, this movement brings together citizens, residents, environmental organisations and activists who see parts of Albania being turned into luxury real estate for elites and a small circle of local intermediaries.

That is why the flamingo has become such a powerful symbol: not only of environmental protection, but also of resistance to the arrogance of power.

Have you heard what's brewing in #Albania ? 🦩 
 
Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump swam ashore from a yacht. They saw an island and thought it needed to “realize its potential”. 

That island is Sazan: a protected natural reserve of the Albanian coast. 
It is home to #flamingos, Mediterannean monk seals and 200 species of migratory birds. To top this off, scientists and local organizations have worked years on protecting this island and its ecosystem. 

But these modern-day “explorers” have other plans: build a luxury resort with over 6,000 rooms! Government even rewrote environmental laws to make this project a reality. 🤯 🤯 

In response, Albanians took the streets to protect what belongs to Albania. They call it the Flamingo Revolution. 🦩 

But here’s what the headlines miss. 

Protesters aren’t fighting a resort. They are fighting for yet another case where communities are excluded from decisions shaping their own future. 

Press always coins this “#Conversation VS #Economy” but it’s a trap that always benefits the same people. 

The real question isn’t choosing between the ocean and community livelihoods. It’s how to build place-based strategies where that choice never has to be made.

Far from headlines,  communities are working hand in hand to build #systems where governance, environment, economy are built in together protecting ocean assets while securing a livelihood for locals. 

These models exist. They are built from the ground up over the years. They just don't arrive on yachts.

And that’s the problem. 

These stories don’t trend. These communities building new system approaches are the least visible to capital. So funding flow where the stories are the loudest: funding extractive models that make communities dependant on one economic activity while taking all of the intrinsic value from the place: its traditional knowledge, its relationships and its future. 

When capital is built around a territory, it amplifies what is already there and innovation serves the vision of the community. 

Part of my work focuses on making coastal communities investable without destroying what is theirs: building places so rooted in systemic value that extraction becomes the irrational choice.
